items.title: Miasma Worm Campaign Spreads with New PyPI Wave
items.description: A coordinated PyPI compromise campaign involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages was detected, utilizing Python startup hooks to execute credential-stealing payloads. The attack leverages .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup, downloads the Bun JavaScript runtime, and runs obfuscated JavaScript payloads. The malware targets high-value developer and CI
items.ai_summary: The Miasma Worm Campaign is a coordinated attack targeting Python developers and CI environments through compromised PyPI packages, utilizing 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages. The attack exploits Python startup hooks to execute credential-stealing payloads, leveraging .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup. If exploited, the malware downloads the Bun JavaScript runtime and runs obfuscated JavaScript payloads, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. To remediate, developers should inspect their Python environments for suspicious .pth files and remove any unknown or untrusted packages, ensuring they are using the latest versions of affected packages and monitoring for unusual activity. Additionally, implementing strict package validation and verification processes can help prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.007| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1550| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Technical Advisory: Breach of Instructure Canvas LMS
items.description: In early May 2026, Instructure confirmed a breach affecting its Canvas learning platform after detecting unauthorized activity on May 1. ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, compromising the Canvas platform directly and exposing names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages. The exposure window ran from April 30 to May 7, 2026. ShinyHunters claims 3.6 TB of data
items.ai_summary: The breach of Instructure Canvas LMS, a popular learning management system, occurred between April 30 and May 7, 2026, when ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, directly compromising the Canvas platform. This exposure resulted in the leakage of sensitive information including names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages, with ShinyHunters claiming to have obtained 3.6 TB of data. The breach is particularly concerning given the educational context, potentially affecting students and educators worldwide. To mitigate similar risks, educational institutions using Canvas should review their account management practices, ensuring that all accounts, especially those created under programs like Free-For-Teacher, adhere to strict verification and monitoring protocols. Institutions should also consider implementing additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication for all users and regularly auditing account activity for suspicious behavior.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1615| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fighting Spyware: An Update
items.description: WhatsApp successfully identified and disrupted spear phishing attempts linked to NSO Group, a spyware firm blacklisted by the US government. The company is requesting the court to hold NSO in contempt for violating a permanent injunction that prohibited them from targeting WhatsApp and its users. The attacks involved social engineering attempts to trick users into clicking malicious links, as well
items.ai_summary: The WhatsApp incident involved spear phishing attempts linked to NSO Group, a spyware firm, which utilized social engineering tactics to trick users into clicking malicious links. The attack vector involved T1566 phishing and T1204 user execution, targeting WhatsApp users. If exploited, the attacks could have resulted in the installation of spyware, potentially affecting user privacy and security. To remediate, WhatsApp users should be cautious of suspicious links and verify the authenticity of messages before clicking on them. Additionally, users can update their WhatsApp application to the latest version and enable two-factor authentication to enhance security.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades Worms Target Bioinformatics and MCP Developers via Malicious PyPI Wheels
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign has expanded to 471 affected artifacts across npm and PyPI, targeting developers through malicious packages. The campaign uses three distinct delivery methods: executable .pth startup hooks, trojanized native .abi3.so extensions that execute at import time, and a split loader-payload architecture that searches Python's sys.path. Twenty-three newly ident
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades Worms campaign is a sophisticated supply chain attack targeting bioinformatics and MCP developers through malicious PyPI wheels. The attack uses three distinct delivery methods, including executable .pth startup hooks, trojanized native .abi3.so extensions, and a split loader-payload architecture that searches Python's sys.path. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and systems, with potential real-world impact on research and development in the bioinformatics and MCP fields. To remediate, developers should verify the authenticity of PyPI packages, avoid using untrusted or unverified packages, and implement secure coding practices, such as input validation and secure dependency management. Additionally, developers should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and update their dependencies to the latest versions, such as Python 3.9 or later, to mitigate the risk of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1550| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A First Look at a New Post-Exploitation Red Team Tool
items.description: A new post-exploitation red team tool named Splinter has been discovered on customer systems through Advanced WildFire's memory scanning capabilities. Developed in Rust programming language, Splinter is exceptionally large at around 7MB due to statically linked libraries. The tool uses a JSON configuration structure containing implant ID, C2 server details, and operational parameters. It operates
items.ai_summary: The newly discovered post-exploitation red team tool, Splinter, is a large 7MB tool developed in Rust, utilizing a JSON configuration structure to store implant ID, C2 server details, and operational parameters. Splinter operates using the Application Layer Protocol, as indicated by the detected TTP T1071, allowing it to potentially evade detection and persist on compromised systems. If exploited, this could lead to significant real-world impact, including unauthorized access and data breaches, particularly for organizations with systems that have been compromised by Splinter. To remediate, defenders should implement specific network traffic monitoring for unusual Application Layer Protocol activity and update their threat detection rules to include signatures for Splinter's large size and distinctive JSON configuration structure. Additionally, defenders should ensure their systems are configured to detect and block unknown or suspicious implants and C2 communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AI brands as bait: How threat actors are using the AI hype in social engineering
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly leveraging the global interest in artificial intelligence by impersonating popular AI platforms such as ChatGPT, Copilot, DeepSeek, and Claude in social engineering campaigns. These operations span phishing attacks, malvertising, and search engine optimization-driven tactics that ultimately lead to credential theft, financial fraud, or malware infections. Observed ca
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are leveraging the global interest in artificial intelligence to impersonate popular AI platforms such as ChatGPT, Copilot, DeepSeek, and Claude in social engineering campaigns. These campaigns involve phishing attacks, malvertising, and search engine optimization-driven tactics, ultimately leading to credential theft, financial fraud, or malware infections. The attack vector typically involves T1566 phishing, where users are tricked into divulging sensitive information, followed by T1204 user execution, where users inadvertently execute malicious code. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on identifying phishing attempts, and ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly web browsers, to prevent T1189 drive-by compromise. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on advertisements, as T1583.008 malvertising can lead to unintended consequences.

    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic

    C -->|T1189| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583.008| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Hacktivists are broadening their scope beyond political motivation
items.description: Kaspersky researchers uncovered interconnected hacktivist campaigns attributed to groups including 4BID, Hakerskii Kit, and C.A.S., targeting organizations primarily in Russia and Belarus, but expanding to Kazakhstan, UAE, Syria, and Egypt. Attackers exploited ProxyShell vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers to deploy fd.aspx web shells and various post-exploitation frameworks including Sl
items.ai_summary: The hacktivist groups, including 4BID, Hakerskii Kit, and C.A.S., have been targeting organizations primarily in Russia and Belarus, but are expanding their scope to other countries such as Kazakhstan, UAE, Syria, and Egypt. The attackers are exploiting ProxyShell vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers, which allows them to deploy fd.aspx web shells and various post-exploitation frameworks, including Sl. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and disruption of services. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure their Microsoft Exchange servers are updated with the latest security patches and configure their systems to restrict access to the fd.aspx file. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505| B[Server Software Component]:::tactic
    B -->|T1491| C[Defacement]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Don't Fear the Repo: UNK_DeadDrop Phishing Campaign Targets Developers to Steal Cryptocurrency
items.description: Between April and May 2026, a likely North Korean threat actor conducted phishing campaigns targeting developers across nearly 100 organizations in finance, cryptocurrency, education, and technology sectors. The attacks used recruitment and code review themes, delivering emails with links to actor-controlled GitHub repositories hosting malicious scripts. The infection chain exploited Visual Studio
items.ai_summary: The UNK_DeadDrop phishing campaign, attributed to a likely North Korean threat actor, targeted developers across nearly 100 organizations in finance, cryptocurrency, education, and technology sectors between April and May 2026. The attack vector involved recruitment and code review themed emails with links to actor-controlled GitHub repositories hosting malicious scripts, exploiting Visual Studio. If exploited, the campaign could result in the theft of cryptocurrency, with real-world impact including financial loss for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement email filtering rules to block suspicious links, educate developers on safe coding practices, and monitor GitHub repositories for malicious activity. Additionally, developers should verify the authenticity of code review requests and be cautious when clicking on links from unknown sources.

graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Old WinRAR Flaw Fuels Attacks on Ukraine: How Unmanaged Software Keeps the Door Open
items.description: Two Russia-aligned campaigns continue exploiting CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR path traversal vulnerability patched in July 2025, against Ukrainian organizations through April 2026. SHADOW-EARTH-066 deploys an evolved GIFTEDCROOK information stealer using in-memory DLL loading via direct NT system calls, harvesting browser credentials, session cookies, and documents across 35 file extensions before self
items.ai_summary: A Russia-aligned threat actor is exploiting CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR path traversal vulnerability patched in July 2025, to gain initial access to Ukrainian organizations. The vulnerability allows for in-memory DLL loading via direct NT system calls, enabling the deployment of an evolved GIFTEDCROOK information stealer. The stealer harvests browser credentials, session cookies, and documents across 35 file extensions before self-deleting. To remediate, update WinRAR to version 6.02 or later, and ensure that software is properly configured to prevent path traversal attacks. Additionally, implement web application security measures to protect against T1539 (Steal Web Session Cookie) and T1555.003 (Credentials from Web Browsers) tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1539| C[Steal Web Session Cookie]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.003| D[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Resurrecting Internet Explorer: Threat Actors Using Zero-day Tricks in Internet Shortcut File to Lure Victims (CVE-2024-38112)
items.description: Check Point Research discovered threat actors leveraging novel techniques to execute malicious code on Windows systems by exploiting Internet Explorer's vulnerabilities. The attackers utilized specially crafted .url files that, when opened, would launch IE and visit attacker-controlled URLs. Additionally, they employed a trick to hide the .hta extension, tricking victims into executing malicious c
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in Internet Explorer, tracked as CVE-2024-38112, to execute malicious code on Windows systems. The attack vector involves specially crafted .url files that launch Internet Explorer and visit attacker-controlled URLs, potentially leading to the execution of malicious code. If exploited, this vulnerability could result in the compromise of sensitive information and disruption of business operations, particularly affecting organizations that still use Internet Explorer. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should ensure that Internet Explorer is updated to the latest version and consider implementing additional security controls, such as blocking .url files from untrusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when opening .url files from unknown sources and consider using alternative browsers.

    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-10162: A flaw has been found in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20. This vulnerability affects...
items.description: A flaw has been found in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20. This vulnerability affects the function formSetPassword of the file /goform/formSetPassword. Executing a manipulation of the argument webpage can lead to stack-based buffer overflow. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit has been published and may be used. The vendor explains: "This product has been EOL for 15 years (since 2009). As
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10162 is a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP version 3.10B20. The flaw is located in the function formSetPassword of the file /goform/formSetPassword, which can be exploited remotely. An attacker can manipulate the argument webpage to trigger the overflow, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. Given the product has been End-of-Life (EOL) since 2009, it is unlikely that the vendor will release a patch. As a result, defenders should assume the vulnerability is unpatchable and focus on mitigating the risk by blocking network traffic to the affected device or replacing it with a supported product. Specifically, administrators should configure their firewalls to block incoming traffic on the device's management interface and consider replacing the device with a supported TRENDnet product.

    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

Performing a manipulation of the argument status_statistic results in stack-based buffer overflow. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used. The vendor explains: "This product has been EOL for 15 years (since 2009).
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10161 is a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP version 3.10B20. The vulnerability affects the function formResetStatistic in the file /goform/formResetStatistic, which can be manipulated remotely. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability to execute arbitrary code, potentially leading to remote code execution (RCE). Given the product has been End-of-Life (EOL) since 2009, it is unlikely that TRENDnet will release a patch. Therefore, defenders should assume this vulnerability will remain unpatched. To mitigate this risk, administrators should consider replacing the affected device with a supported, modern alternative.

items.title: CVE-2026-10160: A security vulnerability has been detected in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20. Affec...
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20. Affected by this issue is the function formSetEnableWizard of the file /goform/formSetEnableWizard. Such manipulation of the argument start_wizard leads to stack-based buffer overflow. The attack can be launched remotely. The exploit has been disclosed publicly and may be used. The vendor explains: "This product has been EO
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10160 is a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP version 3.10B20. The vulnerability is located in the function formSetEnableWizard of the file /goform/formSetEnableWizard, which can be manipulated remotely. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a specially crafted argument start_wizard, leading to a stack-based buffer overflow. If exploited, this vulnerability can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the affected device, potentially leading to a complete compromise of the system. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 3.10B21 or later, which has been patched by the vendor. Additionally, users should ensure that the device is configured to only allow trusted sources to access the formSetEnableWizard function.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10159 is a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in the TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20 router's formSysLog function. The vulnerability is caused by manipulating the argument current_page, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code remotely. This exploit has been made publicly available, posing a significant threat to users of the affected router model. To remediate this issue, users should update to version 3.10B21 or later, which addresses the vulnerability. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the router's firmware is set to automatically update to the latest version.

items.title: ServiceNow discloses security incident exposing customer data
items.description: ServiceNow is warning about a security incident after attackers exploited an unauthenticated access flaw through a vulnerable API endpoint, allowing them to query data from customer instances. [...]

items.description: "Ghost-Sender" uses Exchange Online or on-premises in hybrid mode with a third-party mail server or spam filter to achieve this level of spoofing.

items.description: SAP has released fixes for 15 vulnerabilities as part of its June 2026 Security Patch package, including four critical-severity flaws affecting SAP NetWeaver and SAP Commerce Cloud. [...]

items.title: Adobe Patches 123 Vulnerabilities
items.description: Nearly half of the security holes, most allowing arbitrary code execution, have been fixed in Adobe’s Experience Manager product. The post Adobe Patches 123 Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes 3 zero-day, 200 flaws
items.description: Today is Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday, with security updates for 200 flaws and three publicly disclosed zero-day vulnerabilities. [...]

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: Windows 11 KB5094126 & KB5093998 cumulative updates released
items.description: Microsoft has released Windows 11 KB5094126 and KB5093998 cumulative updates for versions 25H2/24H2 and 23H2 to fix security vulnerabilities, bugs, and add new features. [...]

items.title: Meta to Use Off-Site Business Data for Feed and AI Personalization
items.description: Meta on Tuesday announced that it will use information shared by other businesses to personalize users' feed and responses from its artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot, expanding its scope beyond targeted ads. "Businesses often share information about people's activity on their sites with us to make ads more relevant," Meta said in a statement. "We already use this data - like games you play

items.title: Anthropic Launches Claude Fable 5: Mythos-Class AI With Cybersecurity Guardrails
items.description: The AI giant also announced that Project Glasswing partners are being given access to the upgraded Mythos 5. The post Anthropic Launches Claude Fable 5: Mythos-Class AI With Cybersecurity Guardrails appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: OpenSSL Patches High-Severity Vulnerability Found With AI
items.description: A total of 18 vulnerabilities have been patched in the latest OpenSSL releases, including many that were potentially discovered by AI. The post OpenSSL Patches High-Severity Vulnerability Found With AI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Veeam Backup & Replication RCE Flaw Lets Domain Users Run Remote Code
items.description: Veeam has released security patches to address a critical flaw in its Backup & Replication software that could result in remote code execution. Tracked as CVE-2026-44963, the vulnerability carries a CVSS score of 9.4 out of a maximum of 10.0. "A vulnerability allowing remote code execution (RCE) on the Backup Server by an authenticated domain user," Veeam said in a Tuesday advisory. It

items.title: Microsoft Restores Some GitHub Repos, Keeps Others Offline as Miasma Probe Continues
items.description: Microsoft on Monday confirmed that it temporarily removed some GitHub repositories in response to a recent security incident that led to 73 of its open-source projects being compromised to inject an information stealer into the code. "Our priority is to protect customers and the broader ecosystem," a Microsoft spokesperson told The Hacker News via email. "We temporarily removed some

items.title: XBOW tests Anthropic's Mythos Preview for offensive security
items.description: Anthropic's Mythos Preview was highly effective at finding vulnerability candidates, especially when analyzing source code. XBOW explores how the model performed across exploit discovery, reverse engineering, and live-site validation. [...]

items.title: GitHub disables Microsoft repos pushing password-stealing malware
items.description: Microsoft removed 73 repositories across its Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations on GitHub, disrupting continuous integration pipelines. [...]

items.title: Russian Attackers Weaponize WinRAR Flaw Against Ukrainian Orgs
items.description: Two separate campaigns target CVE-2025-8088, fixed last July, to conduct data theft and cyberespionage against military and government targets in Ukraine.

items.title: Claude Mythos Turns N-Days Into N-Hours With Rapid Exploit Creation
items.description: Public LLM models with safeguards turned off can also build working exploits, increasing patch gap risks. The post Claude Mythos Turns N-Days Into N-Hours With Rapid Exploit Creation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Veeam vulnerability exposes backup servers to RCE attacks
items.description: Veeam has released security updates to patch a critical Backup & Replication security flaw that can be exploited to gain remote code execution (RCE) on domain-joined backup servers. [...]

items.title: Hackers pose as women seeking romance to spy on Russian soldiers
items.description: The group, dubbed SiribClone by Russian cybersecurity firm F6, has been active since at least the summer of 2025 and has primarily targeted members of the Russian armed forces stationed in border regions and combat zones.

items.title: New Platform Uses Cryptographic Invisibility to Protect AI-Built Applications
items.description: Atsign’s AI Architect applies cryptographic protections to agentic software development, aiming to prevent attackers from exploiting vulnerabilities by making application identities effectively invisible. The post New Platform Uses Cryptographic Invisibility to Protect AI-Built Applications appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: WinRAR Flaw Exploited by Russia-Aligned Groups to Deploy Stealers in Ukraine
items.description: Two Russia-aligned cyber attack campaigns have continued to exploit a security flaw in WinRAR to target Ukrainian organisations, almost a year after patches for the vulnerability were released. The activity has been attributed by Trend Micro to Earth Dahu (aka Gamaredon) and SHADOW-EARTH-066 (aka UAC-0226). It involves the exploitation of CVE-2025-8088, a path traversal flaw that allows an

items.title: SAP Patches Critical NetWeaver, Commerce Vulnerabilities
items.description: The flaws could lead to the disclosure of sensitive information, memory corruption, and disruption of normal system usage. The post SAP Patches Critical NetWeaver, Commerce Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
Researchers Build Self-Replicating AI Worm That Operates Entirely on Local, Open-Weight Models
items.description: University of Toronto researchers have built and tested a proof-of-concept AI-driven computer worm that uses a locally hosted open-weight large language model to reason its way through a network, generate tailored attack strategies for each target it encounters, and replicate itself, all without human intervention and without touching a commercial AI service. The preprint, posted to arXiv on

items.title: Chrome V8 Zero-Day CVE-2026-11645 Exploited in the Wild - Patch Now
items.description: Google has released security updates to address 74 vulnerabilities, including one that has come under active exploitation in the wild. The high-severity vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-11645 (CVSS score: 8.8), has been described as an out-of-bounds memory access in V8, Chrome's JavaScript and WebAssembly engine. "Out-of-bounds read and write in V8 in Google Chrome prior to 149.0.7827.103

items.title: Over 100 NPM, PyPI Packages Hit in New Shai-Hulud Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: The most recent variants of the self-propagating attacks are named Miasma and Hades. The post Over 100 NPM, PyPI Packages Hit in New Shai-Hulud Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Hidden Security Risk in Modern Networks: The Work Between Tools
items.description: Organizations have more visibility than ever. Growing tech stacks provide greater coverage, and network security teams are increasingly adopting AI and automation to help with routine tasks and reduce manual effort. But the same challenges persist. Outages still last hours, causing significant financial losses, operational disruption, and reputational impact. Threat response and mean time to

items.title: Will AI Kill the Bug Bounty Industry?
items.description: Anthropic's Mythos is accelerating vulnerability discovery to machine speed, forcing the bug bounty industry and offensive security teams to adapt to a future where finding flaws is no longer the hard part. The post Will AI Kill the Bug Bounty Industry? appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: French govt messaging service breached in account hijacking attack
items.description: DINUM, the digital affairs directorate of the French government, warned that hackers used a hijacked user account to breach Tchap, the French government's encrypted messaging platform. [...]

items.title: New FROST Attack Lets Websites Track What Sites and Apps You Open via SSD Timing
items.description: A malicious website can work out which sites you visit and which apps you open, using nothing but JavaScript and the timing of your SSD. The attack, called FROST, needs no native code, no extension, and no permission prompt. You open the page, leave the tab sitting there, and it watches the drive for contention in the background. Researchers at Graz University of Technology built it and

items.title: Check Point VPN Zero-Day Exploited in Qilin Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The authentication bypass vulnerability allows attackers to establish VPN connections without a valid password. The post Check Point VPN Zero-Day Exploited in Qilin Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hades PyPI Attack: 19 Packages Poisoned to Auto-Run Bun Credential Stealer
items.description: The Miasma supply chain campaign has sparked a fresh attack wave called Hades, this time involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages in the Python Package Index (PyPI) registry, as the Mini Shai-Hulud-style attacks continue to be refined and splintered to target specific ecosystems. "The compromised releases shipped a *-setup.pth file that attempts to execute automatically

items.title: LiteLLM Flaw CVE-2026-42271 Exploited in the Wild, Chains to Unauthenticated RCE
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Monday added a high-severity flaw impacting BerriAI LiteLLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-42271 (CVSS score: 8.7), is a command injection vulnerability that could allow any authenticated user to run arbitrary commands on the

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Silent Ransom Group Hits US Law Firms in Escalating Extortion Attacks
items.description: The financially motivated group is combining vishing, IT impersonation, and in-person office intrusions to steal data and extort victims.

items.title: Check Point VPN Flaw Exploited Since Early May
items.description: A newly discovered, critical zero-day vulnerability is under attack; a Qilin ransomware affiliate has been blamed for at least one incident.

items.title: UK gives big tech 3 months to create device controls to block nude images of kids
items.description: The companies “must activate built-in features or implement technical solutions on smartphones and tablets to detect and block nude images for children,” according to a press release from the Home Office. Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced the measure in a speech at London Tech Week Monday.

items.title: Iran Signed a Ceasefire — Its Hackers Didn't
items.description: An extension of the Geneva Conventions could impose restrictions on cyberwarfare under ceasefire conditions and close a major loophole in international conflict.

items.title: Armenia’s pro-Europe party wins election despite Russia-linked disinformation
items.description: Pashinyan's Civil Contract party won nearly 50% of Sunday's vote, defeating the pro-Russian Strong Armenia party led by Russian-Armenian billionaire Samvel Karapetyan, which received around 23% of the vote.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-06-07, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: This diary continues the Internet Storm Center&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s tracking of the TeamPCP supply chain campaign, first documented in the SANS white paper When the Security Scanner Became the Weapon and most recently in the handler diary Activity Through 2026-05-24. Since that update, the story moved into two new places: the United States government, which formally caught up to the campaign, and the wider population of attackers now wielding the Mini Shai-Hulud framework that TeamPCP open-sourced last month.

items.title: WhatsApp says NSO targeted users with spearfishing attacks in violation of court order
items.description: WhatsApp said it is filing a federal court contempt order against NSO for violating a permanent injunction that bars it from mounting attacks against its users.

items.title: 'Hades' Campaign Against PyPI Puts New Spin on Shai-Hulud
items.description: The latest attacks, which hit 37 PyPI wheels and 19 code packages, show a continued evolution of the persistent software supply chain threat.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9962, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Exposed Fuel Tank Gauges Under Attack in the US
items.description: Threat actors are taking advantage of Internet-exposed tank gauges by breaching gas stations, opening the door to disruption.

items.title: Adaptive, Agentic AI Worms Loom as Next Enterprise Threat
items.description: AI worms, or "viruses with wings and brains," adapt to new environments, seek out vulnerabilities, and will likely strike within a year, researchers say.

items.title: Trump AI Order Seeks Voluntary Frontier Model Testing
items.description: The White House's executive order establishes voluntary framework for early government access to frontier models while investing in federal security.

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 4th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9958, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 3rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9956, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
